label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's tackle this question. So, the passage is about Jennifer Nelson having to pay $1500 for using "Happy Birthday to You" in her documentary. The main point here is the copyright ownership of the song by Warner Music Group since 1988, but the documentary is arguing that the song actually belongs to everyone because of its history. The question asks what the history experts' statement can prove about the "Happy Birthday" song. The options are A, B, C, D. Let me read the relevant part again. It says that a number of history experts state there's no record of who wrote the "Happy Birthday to You" lyrics and that they believe the public was singing it long before it was printed and owned by a company. So, the experts are saying that there's no clear authorship for the lyrics and that it was in public use before copyright. This would challenge Warner Music Group's claim over the copyright, right? Because if the song's origin is uncertain and it was in the public domain long before Warner took ownership, then Warner's claim might not be valid. The lawyers are arguing that it belongs to everyone, so the experts' statements support that the song doesn't belong to Warner Music Group. Looking at the options: A) does not belong to Warner Music Group. That seems correct. B) has more than 200 years' history. The song "Good Morning to All" was written in 1893, so the lyrics were added later. The current song is about 120-130 years old, not over 200, so B is not right. C) was initially owned by another company. The passage doesn't mention another company owning it before Warner. It was written by the Hill sisters and then became public, so no, C is probably wrong. D) has always been very popular. The passage says it was being sung by the public long before being printed, but "always" might be too strong. Also, the experts didn't mention popularity, just the ownership
